docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Retno Dammayatri

GIS & Remote Sensing Analyst

Turning complex geospatial data into clear spatial insight | GIS | Remote Sensing | LiDAR

docs.title: About Me
docs.text:
My journey into GIS began with a fascination for how location and spatial relationships help us understand the world around us. With a background in Geodesy and Geomatics Engineering, I developed a strong foundation in spatial data, remote sensing, and geospatial technologies — combining technical precision with a practical approach to analysing, managing, and visualising geographic information.

I have experience transforming complex spatial datasets into meaningful insights — from designing data collection workflows and managing geospatial databases to developing dashboards, analysing spatial information, and creating clear documentation to support decision-making. I enjoy solving spatial challenges, improving data quality, and building efficient workflows that bridge technical analysis with practical outcomes.

Originally from Indonesia, currently based in Brisbane, Australia on Work and Holiday Visa (subclass 462). I'm actively seeking opportunities in geospatial-related fields in Australia and worldwide. If you're working on something interesting, I'd love to hear from you.

View My Projects Download CV

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Built an interactive dashboard to track landscape restoration activities, spatial coverage, and key monitoring indicators, giving the RMEL team and stakeholders real-time visibility into project progress.

Study Area: Africa Duration: Oct 2025 – Jul 2026 Role: Monitoring and Data Program Officer, RMEL Team Status: Completed

docs.title: Methods & Tools
docs.text:
Data Sources

  • Field-collected monitoring data (via ArcGIS Field Maps)
  • Centralized geospatial project database

Processing Steps

  1. Integrated field-collected data from ArcGIS Field Maps into a centralized geospatial database.
  2. Validated and cleaned spatial and attribute data to ensure reliable inputs for reporting.
  3. Designed web maps in ArcGIS Online to visualize restoration activities and spatial coverage.
  4. Built an interactive dashboard in ArcGIS Dashboard to track key monitoring indicators in real time.

Tools Used

Tool Purpose ArcGIS Field Maps Field data collection ArcGIS Online Web map creation and data hosting ArcGIS Pro Cleaning and Spatial Data Processing ArcGIS Dashboard Interactive dashboard for monitoring indicators

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Enabled real-time tracking of restoration activities and spatial coverage for the team and stakeholder.
  • Improved data accuracy and consistency by standardizing field data collection workflows.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Applied spatial overlay analysis and satellite imagery interpretation to identify areas suitable for landscape restoration and carbon project interventions, supporting eligibility assessment for restoration programs across Africa.

Study Area: Africa Duration: Oct 2025 – Jul 2026 Role: Monitoring and Data Program Officer, RMEL Team Status: Completed

docs.title: Methods & Tools
docs.text:
Data Sources

  • Satellite imagery (for vegetation/land cover interpretation)
  • Restoration program eligibility criteria (Gold Standard requirements)

Processing Steps

  1. Interpreted satellite imagery to assess current land cover and vegetation conditions across candidate restoration areas.
  2. Performed spatial overlay analysis in ArcGIS Pro, combining land cover, land use, and program eligibility criteria layers.
  3. Identified and delineated areas suitable for landscape restoration and carbon project interventions.
  4. Cross-checked eligibility outputs against restoration program requirements (e.g. Gold Standard).

Tools Used

Tool Purpose ArcGIS Pro & QGIS Spatial overlay analysis and eligibility mapping Satellite imagery Land cover and vegetation condition assessment

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text: • Identified land parcel that eligible for restoration and carbon project interventions across the study region.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Geocoded patient address data from all branch clinics into spatial format using the Google Maps API to map and visualize patient distribution patterns, providing insight for other departments' marketing and expansion planning.

Study Area: Jakarta, Indonesia Role: GIS Analyst Status: Completed

docs.title: Methods & Tools
docs.text:
Data Sources

  • Patient address records from all branch clinics

Processing Steps

  1. Collected patient address data across all branch clinics.
  2. Geocoded addresses into spatial coordinates using the Google Maps API.
  3. Visualized the geocoded data to map patient distribution and identify spatial patterns.
  4. Cleaned the outlier data

Tools Used

Tool Purpose Google Maps API Geocoding patient addresses into spatial coordinates ArcGIS Pro and Felt Data visualization and mapping

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Revealed spatial clustering of patients around for all Satu Dental Branches
  • Identified underserved areas with limited patient coverage relative to clinic locations.
  • Findings informed marketing and expansion planning discussions for other departments.

docs.title: Dental Clinic Expansion Analysis Using Spatial Analysis and Hexagon
Overview
docs.text:
Used spatial analysis and a hexagonal grid framework to evaluate population density, competitor locations, and demographic demand for potential dental clinic expansion sites, pinpointing areas with strong market opportunity and limited existing coverage.

Study Area: Java and major cities across Indonesia Role: GIS Analyst Status: Completed

docs.title: Methods & Tools
docs.text:
Data Sources

  • Demographic data
  • Point of Interest (POI) data
  • Income data
  • Road network data
  • Competitor (existing dental clinic) locations

Processing Steps

  1. Compiled demographic, income, POI, road, and competitor datasets covering Java and major Indonesian cities.
  2. Built a hexagonal grid over the study area to standardize spatial analysis units.
  3. Aggregated and scored each hexagon based on population density, income levels, accessibility (roads), and competitor proximity.
  4. Produced a suitability map ranking hexagons by expansion potential for new dental clinic locations.

Tools Used

Tool Purpose ArcGIS Pro, QGIS Hexagon grid generation and spatial scoring ArcGIS Pro, QGIS Data processing and mapping OpenStreetMap Data Source

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text: • Identified high-suitability hexagon zones across Java and major Indonesian cities for dental clinic expansion.

docs.title: Geospatial Intelligence
docs.text:
A selection of my geospatial intelligence analysis projects. Click any card to see the full write-up.

Nusakambangan Case

As determined by the government, Nusakambangan Island is not intended to be inhabited by civilians — it can only be inhabited by prisoners, prison officials, and their families. However, there have been reports suggesting that parts of the island have been converted for other uses. This study used satellite imagery and Open Source Intelligence (OSINT) to investigate, applying visual analysis of high-resolution satellite imagery for time-series observation and spatial analysis.

View Result →

Pertamina Cilacap Case

On November 13, 2021, a fire occurred in tank 36 T-102 at the Pertamina Cilacap refinery. This study analyzed the incident before and after using satellite imagery (before) and drone-acquired imagery (after) to assess the impact.

View Result →

Cianjur Earthquake Case

On November 21, 2022, a Mw 5.6 earthquake struck the Cianjur area, West Java. This study used archive and newest satellite imagery from Maxar (SecureWatch) to assess change and impact.

View Result →

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Processed airborne LiDAR and photogrammetry data — from raw trajectory and point cloud extraction through to classified elevation models and topographic map layouts — supporting multiple dam, irrigation, and lake topographic survey projects across Indonesia.

Study Area: Multiple sites across Indonesia (Papua, South Sulawesi, West Sumatera) Role: GIS & Remote Sensing Analyst Status: Completed

docs.title: Data Acquisition
docs.text:
LiDAR and airborne data acquisition is using Leica ALS80 and CASA aircraft.

Leica ALS80 CASA Aircraft

docs.title: Methods & Tools
docs.text:
Data Sources

  • Airborne LiDAR sensor data
  • GNSS/trajectory data for georeferencing
  • Aerial photogrammetry imagery

Processing Steps

Pre-Processing

1. Trajectory Processing 2. Raw Data Extraction 3. Strip Adjustment Trajectory is used to reference laser and photo coordinates to ground coordinates according to BM references. Converted from LiDAR sensor format to point cloud. Strip adjustment is done to make corrections between paths, using TerraMatch.

Post-Processing

1. Point Cloud Classification 2. Elevation Modelling 3. Digitizing & Layouting Classified point cloud data into classes: ground and non-ground (vegetation, buildings, water, and noise). Converted the classified data into Digital Elevation Models (DTM & DSM). Digitizing and layouting into topographic map.

Tools Used

Tool Purpose NovAtel Inertial Explorer, Leica HxMap Trajectory and data extraction TerraSolid, MicroStation Point cloud classification and post-processing TerraMatch Strip adjustment ArcGIS, Global Mapper Map digitizing and visualization Leica HxMap Photogrammetry processing Emlid Studio, RTKLIB GNSS PPK/trajectory processing

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
Delivered airborne LiDAR topographic surveys and mapping for the following projects:

  • Topographic Survey and Mapping for the Development of Digoel Multipurpose Dam — Digoel, South Papua, Indonesia | Jul 2021 | View Sample
  • Topographic Survey and Mapping for the Development of Rongkong Multipurpose Dam — Luwu, South Sulawesi, Indonesia | Jan 2022 | View Sample
  • Topographic Survey and Mapping for the Development of Warsamson Multipurpose Dam — Sorong, West Papua, Indonesia | Jul 2021 | View Sample
  • Lake Maninjau Topographic Survey and Mapping — Lake Maninjau, West Sumatera, Indonesia | Jun 2021 | View Sample
  • Topographic Survey and Mapping for the Development of The Merauke Irrigation System — Merauke, South Papua, Indonesia | Feb 2021 | View Sample

A remote sensing project that I have done using Google Earth Engine to monitor mangrove loss over time. The analysis used Landsat imagery from 2000, 2010, and 2020 and applied Random Forest Classification to map and assess changes in mangrove extent.

DSM & DTM Generation From Stereo Pair High-Resolution Satellite Imagery

Projects that I have done include generating DSM and DTM from 50 cm WorldView-3 stereo imagery for Lake Toba, Indonesia. The project used ERDAS Imagine for stereo processing and triangulation, and PCI Geomatica for terrain extraction and surface-object removal.

docs.title: Data Acquisition

DJI Matrice 300 RTK GNSS Surveying

docs.title: Methods & Tools

docs.title: DTM and DSM Generation from High-Resolution Stereo Satellite Imagery – Lake Toba
Overview
docs.text: This project generated a Digital Terrain Model (DTM) and Digital Surface Model (DSM) for the Lake Toba area, Indonesia, using high-resolution stereo satellite imagery — an efficient approach for producing elevation models over large areas.

docs.title: Study Area: Lake Toba, Indonesia (208,500 Ha)
Methods & Tools
docs.text:
Data Sources

  • WorldView-3 high-resolution stereo pair satellite imagery (50 cm resolution)

Processing Steps

  1. Acquired a stereo pair of WorldView-3 images (two images of the same area from different viewpoints) to enable 3D reconstruction.
  2. Generated tie points between the two images in ERDAS Imagine.
  3. Performed triangulation on the tie points to extract the Digital Surface Model (DSM).
  4. Processed the DSM in PCI Geomatica to remove above-surface objects (e.g., buildings, vegetation) and produce the Digital Terrain Model (DTM).

Tools Used

Tool Purpose ERDAS Imagine Tie point generation and triangulation for DSM extraction PCI Geomatica Filtering above-surface features to derive the DTM WorldView-3 stereo imagery Source 3D data for elevation modeling

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Produced a DTM and DSM covering the full 208,500 Ha Lake Toba study area from a single WorldView-3 stereo pair.
  • Demonstrated that stereo satellite imagery is an efficient method for elevation modeling over large areas compared to ground-based surveying.
  • [Add a specific finding, e.g. vertical accuracy, elevation range, or comparison to a reference DEM]

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This project analyzed changes in Green Open Space (GOS) across DKI Jakarta Province in 2014, 2015, and 2019 to help understand the link between shrinking green open space, reduced water catchment area, and Jakarta's recurring flooding problems.

Study Area: DKI Jakarta Province, Indonesia

docs.title: Methods & Tools
docs.text:
Data Sources

  • Landsat 8 OLI/TIRS imagery (Level L1TP), years 2014, 2015, and 2019

Processing Steps

  1. Acquired Landsat 8 OLI/TIRS L1TP imagery for 2014, 2015, and 2019.
  2. Calculated the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) for each year using ENVI and ArcGIS Desktop.
  3. Classified the NDVI results into specific vegetation/green open space classes.
  4. Produced green open space distribution maps for DKI Jakarta for each of the three years and compared them to identify change over time.

Tools Used

Tool Purpose ENVI NDVI calculation from Landsat imagery ArcGIS Desktop NDVI classification and map production Landsat 8 OLI/TIRS Source satellite imagery for 2014, 2015, and 2019

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Green open space area in DKI Jakarta declined across all three years studied (2014, 2015, 2019).
  • Green open space dropped significantly by 2.9% between 2014 and 2015.
  • Green open space declined by a further 0.8% between 2015 and 2019.

docs.title: Links
docs.text: View Article on Medium

docs.title: Mangrove Loss Monitoring in Muaragembong Using Google Earth Engine
docs.text: View Google Earth Engine App

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This project monitors mangrove cover change in Muaragembong over time, using Google Earth Engine (GEE) to identify areas of mangrove loss and gain and to better understand long-term trends in mangrove extent.

Study Area: Muaragembong, Bekasi, West Java, Indonesia

docs.title: Methods & Tools
docs.text:
Data Sources

  • Landsat imagery for the years 2000, 2010, and 2020

Processing Steps

  1. Acquired Landsat imagery for 2000, 2010, and 2020 in Google Earth Engine.
  2. Calculated the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) for each time period, where higher NDVI values indicate higher levels of vegetation (mangroves).
  3. Trained a Random Forest classifier using the NDVI-derived inputs to classify mangrove vs. non-mangrove cover for each year.
  4. Compared classified outputs across the three time periods to identify areas of mangrove loss and gain.

Tools Used

Tool Purpose Google Earth Engine (GEE) Image processing, NDVI calculation, and Random Forest classification Landsat imagery Source satellite data for 2000, 2010, and 2020 Random Forest Classification Classifying mangrove vs. non-mangrove areas from NDVI

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Produced a multi-temporal (2000, 2010, 2020) map of mangrove extent for the study area.
  • Identified spatial patterns of mangrove loss and gain across the three time periods.
  • Delivered results as an interactive Google Earth Engine App for public viewing.

docs.title: Land Cover Change Analysis – Trans Sumatera Toll Road, Palembang
docs.text:
NDBI built-up area classification results, 2017–2019

Explore Interactive WebGIS Results View Full Story on ArcGIS StoryMaps

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Analyzed land cover change around the Jakabaring toll gate in Seberang Ulu, Palembang, to assess how Trans Sumatera Toll Road development is driving the area's growth in line with the city government's development plan.

Study Area: Seberang Ulu (Jakabaring Toll Gate area), Palembang — 85.642 km²

docs.title: Methods & Tools
docs.text:
Data Sources

  • Sentinel-2 Level 1C imagery, 2017, 2018, and 2019

Processing Steps

  1. Calculated the Normalized Difference Built-up Index (NDBI) from Sentinel-2 imagery to map built-up areas.
  2. Classified NDBI results into four density classes (non built-up, low, medium, high density built-up), then simplified into two main classes: non built-up and built-up.
  3. Compared built-up extent across 2017–2019 to analyze the toll road's impact on development growth.

Tools Used

Tool Purpose Sentinel-2 imagery Source data for NDBI analysis (2017–2019) NDBI (Normalized Difference Built-up Index) Mapping and classifying built-up areas Web GIS Publishing interactive results

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Built-up land in Seberang Ulu grew by 6.15% between 2017 and 2019.
  • Growth aligns with the Palembang city government's plan to intensify development in the Seberang Ulu area following Trans Sumatera Toll Road construction.
